schools.summary: Miriam and Aaron speaking against Moses over his wife: 'and Miriam and Aaron spake against Moses because of the Ethiopian woman whom he had married: for he had married an Ethiopian woman' — Miriam and Aaron, Moses's own brother and sister, spoke against him, taking occasion from the Ethiopian (Cushite) woman he had married (the spake against Moses, the murmuring against God's chosen servant, the because of the Ethiopian woman whom he had married, the pretext, the marriage made the occasion, the figure, the sad sight of the nearest kin turning against God's servant, envy rising even in the family, the pretext of the marriage masking the deeper grievance of envy at Moses's place, vv.2, the murmuring against the man God had set over them, the warning that even the godly may fall into envy and detraction, speaking against the Lord's anointed, Psalm 105:15); Moses's brother and sister speaking against him (the murmuring of the kin) — the sedition (the speaking against God's servant); 'because of the Ethiopian woman whom he had married' (the marriage the occasion, the pretext for envy, Psalm 105:15, the figure, the deeper grievance masked, the providence, the murmuring, the claim of equal prophecy to follow, vv.2) — Henry, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And Miriam and Aaron spake against Moses because of the Ethiopian woman whom he had married: for he had married an Ethiopian woman
Miriam and Aaron speaking against Moses over his wife
the nearest kin turning against God's chosen servant, the marriage the pretext
the deeper envy at Moses's place, the warning against speaking against the Lord's anointed (Psalm 105:15)

schools.summary: The claim of equal prophecy and the LORD hearing it: 'and they said, Hath the LORD indeed spoken only by Moses? hath he not spoken also by us? And the LORD heard it' — the real grievance: Miriam and Aaron envied Moses's unique authority, claiming they too were prophets; and the LORD heard their words (the Hath the LORD indeed spoken only by Moses?, the challenge to Moses's unique standing, the hath he not spoken also by us?, the claim of equal prophecy, Miriam a prophetess, Exodus 15:20, and Aaron the priest, the And the LORD heard it, God hearing the murmuring, the secret grievance not hidden from him, the figure, the envy of God's servant's special place, the grasping at equal honor, the pride that will not bear another's pre-eminence, the truth that God hears the murmuring of the heart, 'the LORD heard it,' nothing hidden from him, Malachi 3:16, the danger of envy at the gifts and place God gives to others); the claim of equal prophecy (the envy of Moses's place, Exodus 15:20) — the envy (the grasping at honor); 'And the LORD heard it' (God hearing the murmuring, nothing hidden, Malachi 3:16, the figure, the secret grievance known to God, the providence, the LORD hearing, the vindication of Moses to follow, vv.4-8) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And they said, Hath the LORD indeed spoken only by Moses? hath he not spoken also by us? And the LORD heard it
the claim of equal prophecy and the LORD hearing it
the envy of Moses's unique place, the grasping at equal honor (Exodus 15:20)

schools.summary: The meekness of Moses above all men: '(now the man Moses was very meek, above all the men which were upon the face of the earth.)' — the meekness — 'the man Moses was very meek, above all the men which were upon the face of the earth': in the midst of the attack on him, the Spirit recording that Moses was the meekest of all men, that his vindication came not from himself but from God (the witnesses noting the significance — the surpassing meekness of Moses, not defending himself, not resenting the wrong, leaving his cause to God, the lesson — that the meekness which does not avenge itself but leaves all to God is the highest grace, Moses, so provoked by his own kin, made no reply, but God arose to vindicate him, the meek who do not return the injury but commit themselves to the righteous Judge, the question of the authorship — some noting that Moses, the writer, recording his own meekness, is no contradiction, for he wrote as moved by the Spirit, setting down the truth God testified, as he recorded his own faults elsewhere, the figure — Moses the meek a figure of Christ, 'I am meek and lowly in heart,' Matthew 11:29, 'who, when he was reviled, reviled not again... but committed himself to him that judgeth righteously,' 1 Peter 2:23, the meekness that inherits the earth, 'blessed are the meek: for they shall inherit the earth,' Matthew 5:5, the figure of the servant of God who endures wrong without retaliation, leaving his cause to God, supremely fulfilled in Christ the meek and lowly, the figure of meekness vindicated by God); Moses very meek (not avenging himself, leaving his cause to God, 1 Peter 2:23) — the meekness (the highest grace); Moses the meekest of all (the surpassing meekness, the figure of Christ, Matthew 11:29) — the figure (the meek who inherit the earth, Matthew 5:5, the providence, the meekness recorded, God's vindication to follow) — Calvin, Keil, Wesley, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the testimony of Moses's meekness teach?

sides.position: The surpassing meekness that does not avenge itself but commits all to God (1 Peter 2:23) - the highest grace, a figure of Christ the meek and lowly who when reviled reviled not again (Matthew 11:29; Matthew 5:5), the servant of God vindicated by God

themes: (Now the man Moses was very meek, above all the men which were upon the face of the earth.)
the meekness of Moses above all men (the crux of the meekness that figures Christ)
the surpassing grace that does not avenge itself but commits all to God (1 Peter 2:23)
a figure of Christ the meek and lowly, the servant vindicated by God (Matthew 11:29; Matthew 5:5)

schools.summary: The LORD suddenly calling the three to the tabernacle: 'and the LORD spake suddenly unto Moses, and unto Aaron, and unto Miriam, Come out ye three unto the tabernacle of the congregation. And they three came out' — the LORD suddenly summoned all three to the tabernacle, to judge the matter himself (the the LORD spake suddenly, the swift summons, God acting at once for his servant, the Come out ye three unto the tabernacle, all three called to God's presence, the And they three came out, their obedience to the summons, the figure, God arising suddenly to vindicate his servant, the swiftness of his defense of the wronged, the summons before God who judges the matter, the truth that God himself takes up the cause of his servant, the wronged need not defend himself when God arises, Romans 12:19, the three brought before the LORD for judgment); the sudden summons of the three (God acting for his servant) — the summons (the swift call); 'Come out ye three... And they three came out' (all three called and coming, the summons obeyed, Romans 12:19, the figure, the cause taken up by God, the providence, the three before God, the vindication to follow, vv.5-8) — Henry, Gill.

themes: And the LORD spake suddenly unto Moses, and unto Aaron, and unto Miriam, Come out ye three unto the tabernacle of the congregation. And they three came out
the LORD suddenly calling the three to the tabernacle
God arising suddenly to vindicate his servant, the swiftness of his defense
the wronged needing not defend himself when God arises (Romans 12:19)

schools.summary: The LORD coming down in the cloud to judge: 'and the LORD came down in the pillar of the cloud, and stood in the door of the tabernacle, and called Aaron and Miriam: and they both came forth' — the LORD came down in the pillar of cloud, stood at the tabernacle door, and called Aaron and Miriam forth, apart from Moses (the the LORD came down in the pillar of the cloud, the visible token of God's presence descending to judge, the called Aaron and Miriam, the two offenders called apart, Moses not summoned to hear his own defense, the and they both came forth, the two standing before God, the figure, God descending to judge the matter, the presence in the cloud coming to vindicate his servant, the offenders called apart, that Moses not be present at his own vindication, the solemnity of God himself arising to defend the wronged and rebuke the offenders, the truth that God judges between his servants, the accusers brought before him, Psalm 50:21); God descending in the cloud to judge (the presence coming) — the descent (God coming to judge); 'called Aaron and Miriam: and they both came forth' (the two offenders called apart, Moses not present at his vindication, Psalm 50:21, the figure, God arising to judge, the providence, the offenders before God, the rebuke to follow, vv.6-8) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And the LORD came down in the pillar of the cloud, and stood in the door of the tabernacle, and called Aaron and Miriam: and they both came forth
the LORD coming down in the cloud to judge
God descending to judge the matter, the offenders called apart
the presence coming to vindicate his servant and rebuke the accusers (Psalm 50:21)

schools.summary: God's ordinary way of speaking to prophets by vision and dream: 'and he said, Hear now my words: If there be a prophet among you, I the LORD will make myself known unto him in a vision, and will speak unto him in a dream' — God declared his ordinary way of revealing himself to prophets: by vision and dream, not the close communion he had with Moses (the If there be a prophet among you, the ordinary prophet, the I the LORD will make myself known unto him in a vision, and will speak unto him in a dream, the manner of revelation to the prophets, indirect, by vision and dream, the figure, the lesser, mediated revelation given to the prophets, distinguished from the direct speech with Moses, the gradation of revelation, the prophets receiving by vision and dream, 'God... spake in time past unto the fathers by the prophets,' Hebrews 1:1, the contrast that exalts Moses's closeness, vv.7-8, the manner of prophetic revelation, the foreshadowing of the fuller revelation to come); the ordinary prophet receiving by vision (the mediated revelation, Hebrews 1:1) — the manner (the vision); 'and will speak unto him in a dream' (the prophets spoken to in dreams, the indirect revelation, the figure, the lesser revelation distinguished from Moses's, Hebrews 1:1, the providence, the prophets by vision and dream, Moses's closeness to follow, vv.7-8) — Calvin, Gill.

themes: And he said, Hear now my words: If there be a prophet among you, I the LORD will make myself known unto him in a vision, and will speak unto him in a dream
God's ordinary way of speaking to prophets by vision and dream
the lesser, mediated revelation given to the prophets, distinguished from the direct speech with Moses
God spake in time past to the fathers by the prophets (Hebrews 1:1)

schools.summary: Moses faithful in all God's house: 'my servant Moses is not so, who is faithful in all mine house' — Moses was not like the ordinary prophets, for he was faithful in all God's house, trusted with the whole (the My servant Moses is not so, Moses above the ordinary prophets, the who is faithful in all mine house, the trusted steward, faithful over the whole household of God, the figure, Moses the faithful servant over all God's house, the steward trusted with the whole, the New Testament use, where Moses's faithfulness is the figure that exalts Christ: 'Moses verily was faithful in all his house, as a servant... but Christ as a son over his own house,' Hebrews 3:2-6, Moses faithful as a servant, Christ faithful as the Son and Lord of the house, the figure of the faithful steward, and of Christ the faithful Son over the house of God, the honor God puts on his faithful servant); Moses above the ordinary prophets (not by vision and dream only) — the distinction (Moses unlike the rest); 'who is faithful in all mine house' (Moses the faithful steward of the whole house, Hebrews 3:2-6, the figure, Moses faithful as a servant, Christ as the Son, the providence, the faithful servant, the mouth-to-mouth speech to follow, vv.8) — Clarke, Gill.

themes: My servant Moses is not so, who is faithful in all mine house
Moses faithful in all God's house
Moses the faithful servant over all God's house, the steward trusted with the whole
a figure that exalts Christ the faithful Son over his own house (Hebrews 3:2-6)

schools.summary: God speaking with Moses mouth to mouth and rebuking the offenders: 'with him will I speak mouth to mouth, even apparently, and not in dark speeches; and the similitude of the LORD shall he behold: wherefore then were ye not afraid to speak against my servant Moses?' — the close communion — 'with him will I speak mouth to mouth, even apparently, and not in dark speeches; and the similitude of the LORD shall he behold': God spoke with Moses directly, mouth to mouth, clearly and not in riddles, and Moses beheld the form of the LORD (the witnesses noting the significance — the unique nearness of Moses to God, the direct, clear, face-to-face communion, far above the vision and dream of the ordinary prophets, vv.6, the lesson — that God set Moses in a place of nearness above all, 'the LORD spake unto Moses face to face, as a man speaketh unto his friend,' Exodus 33:11; Deuteronomy 34:10, 'there arose not a prophet since in Israel like unto Moses, whom the LORD knew face to face,' the gravity of the rebuke — 'wherefore then were ye not afraid to speak against my servant Moses?', the offenders rebuked for daring to murmur against the man God so honored, the figure — Moses's nearness a figure of the yet greater nearness of Christ, who is in the bosom of the Father and has declared him, 'no man hath seen God at any time; the only begotten Son... he hath declared him,' John 1:18, the warning against speaking against the Lord's servants, 'touch not mine anointed,' Psalm 105:15, the holy fear due to those God sets near himself, the figure of the danger of detraction against God's honored servants, and of the surpassing nearness of Christ to the Father); the direct, clear speech with Moses, beholding the form of the LORD (the unique nearness, Exodus 33:11; Deuteronomy 34:10) — the communion (the face-to-face nearness); 'wherefore then were ye not afraid to speak against my servant Moses?' (the offenders rebuked for speaking against the honored servant, the holy fear due, Psalm 105:15, the figure, the warning against detraction, John 1:18, the providence, the rebuke, the leprosy of Miriam to follow, vv.9-10) — Ellicott, Wesley, Gill.

debates.question: What does God's mouth-to-mouth speech with Moses establish?

sides.position: The unique nearness of Moses to God, the direct clear communion far above the prophets' vision and dream (Exodus 33:11; Deuteronomy 34:10) - and the grave rebuke of those who dared speak against the honored servant (Psalm 105:15), a figure of the surpassing nearness of Christ in the bosom of the Father (John 1:18)

themes: With him will I speak mouth to mouth, even apparently, and not in dark speeches; and the similitude of the LORD shall he behold: wherefore then were ye not afraid to speak against my servant Moses?
God speaking with Moses mouth to mouth and rebuking the offenders (the crux of Moses's unique nearness to God)
the direct, clear communion of Moses with God, far above the prophets' vision and dream (Exodus 33:11; Deuteronomy 34:10)
the grave rebuke of those who speak against his honored servant, a figure of Christ's nearness to the Father (Psalm 105:15; John 1:18)

schools.summary: The anger of the LORD kindled and his departure: 'and the anger of the LORD was kindled against them; and he departed' — God's anger kindled against Miriam and Aaron, and the cloud of his presence departed (the the anger of the LORD was kindled against them, the wrath at their sin against his servant, the and he departed, the cloud withdrawing, the token of displeasure, the figure, the anger of God at the murmuring against his servant, the displeasure that follows the sin, the withdrawal of the presence the mark of God's displeasure, the cloud departing, the truth that God is angry at the wrong done to his servants, the sin taken as against himself, the warning of the displeasure that follows detraction and envy, the departing presence); God's anger at the offenders (the displeasure at the sin) — the wrath (the anger kindled); 'and he departed' (the cloud withdrawing, the token of displeasure, the figure, the presence departing in anger, the providence, the anger kindled, Miriam's leprosy to follow, vv.10) — Calvin, Gill.

themes: And the anger of the LORD was kindled against them; and he departed
the anger of the LORD kindled and his departure
the wrath at the murmuring against his servant, the cloud withdrawing the token of displeasure
the sin taken as against God himself

schools.summary: Miriam stricken with leprosy white as snow: 'and the cloud departed from off the tabernacle; and, behold, Miriam became leprous, white as snow: and Aaron looked upon Miriam, and, behold, she was leprous' — as the cloud departed, Miriam struck with leprosy, white as snow, and Aaron saw her (the the cloud departed from off the tabernacle, the presence withdrawn, the Miriam became leprous, white as snow, the judgment, the leprosy of the worst kind, the Aaron looked upon Miriam... she was leprous, the priest beholding the judgment, the figure, the leprosy the visible judgment on the sin, the murmuring punished with the defiling disease, Miriam, the leader in the sin, struck, while Aaron the priest spared, perhaps for his office, yet rebuked by the sight, the figure that sin defiles and is judged, the leprosy a token of the uncleanness of sin, the warning of the judgment on detraction and envy, the visible mark of God's displeasure); Miriam struck with leprosy (the judgment, the leprosy white as snow) — the judgment (the leprosy); 'Aaron looked upon Miriam... she was leprous' (Aaron beholding the judgment, the priest spared but rebuked, the figure, the leprosy the token of sin's uncleanness, the providence, Miriam struck, Aaron's confession to follow, vv.11) — Henry, Gill.

themes: And the cloud departed from off the tabernacle; and, behold, Miriam became leprous, white as snow: and Aaron looked upon Miriam, and, behold, she was leprous
Miriam stricken with leprosy white as snow
the murmuring punished with the defiling disease, the leprosy the visible token of sin's uncleanness
the warning of the judgment on detraction and envy

schools.summary: Aaron's confession and appeal to Moses: 'and Aaron said unto Moses, Alas, my lord, I beseech thee, lay not the sin upon us, wherein we have done foolishly, and wherein we have sinned' — Aaron, humbled, confessed the sin and appealed to Moses, the very man they had wronged, for help (the Alas, my lord, the humble address, Aaron now calling Moses lord, the lay not the sin upon us, the plea for forgiveness, the wherein we have done foolishly, and wherein we have sinned, the honest confession of the folly and the sin, the figure, the offender humbled, confessing the sin and appealing to the one wronged, the change from envy to humility, Aaron now owning Moses as lord, the confession that owns the folly and the sin, the way of repentance, the figure of the proud brought low, the sin confessed, the appeal to the meek servant they had wronged, 'confess your faults one to another,' James 5:16); Aaron's humble appeal to Moses (the plea for forgiveness) — the appeal (the plea); 'wherein we have done foolishly, and wherein we have sinned' (the honest confession of the sin, the folly owned, James 5:16, the figure, the way of repentance, the sin confessed, the providence, the confession, Moses's intercession to follow, vv.13) — Calvin, Gill.

themes: And Aaron said unto Moses, Alas, my lord, I beseech thee, lay not the sin upon us, wherein we have done foolishly, and wherein we have sinned
Aaron's confession and appeal to Moses
the proud brought low, the sin and folly confessed
the appeal to the meek servant they had wronged, the way of repentance (James 5:16)

schools.summary: Aaron's pleading for Miriam's life: 'let her not be as one dead, of whom the flesh is half consumed when he cometh out of his mother's womb' — Aaron pleaded that Miriam not be left like a stillborn child, half consumed, for the leprosy was wasting her (the Let her not be as one dead, the plea against the wasting death, the of whom the flesh is half consumed when he cometh out of his mother's womb, the figure of the leprosy consuming the flesh as a stillbirth, the figure, the dreadful wasting of the leprosy likened to a corrupt stillbirth, the flesh consumed, the plea for mercy on the stricken, the appeal to spare her from the consuming disease, the figure of the corruption and death that sin works, the flesh consumed, a vivid picture of the wasting of the body under the judgment, the cry for life against the consuming death of the leprosy); the plea against the wasting death (the leprosy consuming) — the plea (against the death); 'the flesh is half consumed when he cometh out of his mother's womb' (the leprosy consuming the flesh as a stillbirth, the wasting disease, the figure, the corruption sin works on the flesh, the providence, the plea for Miriam, Moses's prayer to follow, vv.13) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Let her not be as one dead, of whom the flesh is half consumed when he cometh out of his mother's womb
Aaron's pleading for Miriam's life
the dreadful wasting of the leprosy likened to a corrupt stillbirth, the flesh consumed

schools.summary: Moses's prayer for the healing of Miriam: 'and Moses cried unto the LORD, saying, Heal her now, O God, I beseech thee' — the prayer — 'Moses cried unto the LORD, saying, Heal her now, O God, I beseech thee': Moses, the very one Miriam had wronged, cried to God to heal her, with a brief and earnest prayer (the witnesses noting the significance — the meek servant interceding for the one who had wronged him, Moses, so far from resenting Miriam's sin, prayed at once for her healing, the lesson — the highest expression of the meekness recorded in vv.3, the wronged praying for the wrongdoer, returning good for evil, the love that intercedes for its enemies, the brevity and earnestness of the prayer, 'Heal her now, O God, I beseech thee' — the short, fervent cry of love, the figure — Moses a figure of Christ, who prayed for his enemies, 'Father, forgive them; for they know not what they do,' Luke 23:34, the love that 'blesses them which persecute you,' 'pray for them which despitefully use you,' Matthew 5:44; Romans 12:14, the intercession of the meek for those who wrong them, supremely fulfilled in Christ who intercedes for his people and prayed for his crucifiers, the figure of forgiving love that turns at once to prayer for the offender, the truth that the meek do not avenge but intercede, the figure of the wronged servant praying for the healing of the one who wronged him); Moses crying to God at once (the earnest, fervent prayer) — the cry (the prayer to God); 'Heal her now, O God, I beseech thee' (Moses praying for the healing of the one who wronged him, the meek interceding for his enemy, Luke 23:34; Matthew 5:44, the figure, the forgiving love, Christ praying for his crucifiers, the providence, the prayer, God's answer to follow, vv.14) — Calvin, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does Moses's prayer for Miriam reveal?

sides.position: The meek servant interceding at once for the one who wronged him, returning good for evil - the highest expression of his surpassing meekness, a figure of Christ who prayed for his enemies and crucifiers (Luke 23:34; Matthew 5:44; Romans 12:14)

themes: And Moses cried unto the LORD, saying, Heal her now, O God, I beseech thee

the wronged praying at once for the wrongdoer, returning good for evil
a figure of Christ who prayed for his enemies and crucifiers (Luke 23:34; Matthew 5:44; Romans 12:14)

schools.summary: God's answer, Miriam shut out seven days: 'and the LORD said unto Moses, If her father had but spit in her face, should she not be ashamed seven days? let her be shut out from the camp seven days, and after that let her be received in again' — God answered that Miriam, though healed, must bear the shame of her sin, shut out of the camp seven days, as a daughter shamed by a father's rebuke (the If her father had but spit in her face, should she not be ashamed seven days?, the figure of a father's open rebuke, the shame to be borne, the let her be shut out from the camp seven days, the exclusion, the bearing of the shame, the after that let her be received in again, the restoration after the chastening, the figure, the chastening that, though the sin is forgiven, requires the bearing of the shame and the discipline, the mercy that heals yet disciplines, the forgiven still bearing the chastisement, the figure that God's children, though pardoned, may be chastened for their sins, 'whom the Lord loveth he chasteneth,' Hebrews 12:6, the shame and exclusion borne, then the restoration, the discipline that humbles before it restores); the figure of the father's rebuke and the shame to be borne (the chastening) — the shame (the discipline); 'let her be shut out from the camp seven days... after that let her be received in again' (the exclusion and then restoration, the chastening, then the receiving back, Hebrews 12:6, the figure, the forgiven still chastened, then restored, the providence, Miriam shut out, the people staying for her, vv.15) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And the LORD said unto Moses, If her father had but spit in her face, should she not be ashamed seven days? let her be shut out from the camp seven days, and after that let her be received in again
God's answer, Miriam shut out seven days
the mercy that heals yet disciplines, the forgiven bearing the shame before the restoration
the chastening that humbles before it restores (Hebrews 12:6)

schools.summary: Miriam shut out and the people staying for her: 'and Miriam was shut out from the camp seven days: and the people journeyed not till Miriam was brought in again' — Miriam bore her seven days' exclusion, and the whole people waited, not journeying until she was restored (the Miriam was shut out from the camp seven days, the chastening borne, the the people journeyed not till Miriam was brought in again, the whole camp waiting for her restoration, the figure, the people staying for the one shut out, the patience of God's people with the chastened, the honor God put on Miriam even in her chastening, the host waiting for her, the figure of the body that waits for its members, none left behind, the weak and the chastened borne with, the truth that God's people are knit together, the whole staying for the one, 'the members should have the same care one for another,' 1 Corinthians 12:25, the patience that waits for the restoration of the fallen); Miriam bearing the seven days' exclusion (the chastening) — the exclusion (the chastening borne); 'the people journeyed not till Miriam was brought in again' (the whole camp waiting for her restoration, the patience with the chastened, 1 Corinthians 12:25, the figure, the body waiting for its members, the providence, Miriam restored, the journey resumed, vv.16) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And Miriam was shut out from the camp seven days: and the people journeyed not till Miriam was brought in again

schools.summary: The journey to the wilderness of Paran: 'and afterward the people removed from Hazeroth, and pitched in the wilderness of Paran' — after Miriam's restoration, the people journeyed from Hazeroth and encamped in the wilderness of Paran (the afterward the people removed from Hazeroth, the journey resumed after the chastening, the and pitched in the wilderness of Paran, the next encampment, the place from which the spies would be sent, Numbers 13:3, the figure, the journey continued after the discipline, the people moving on, the pilgrimage resumed, the camp at Paran whence the spies would go, the truth that God's people, after the chastening, journey on under his guidance, the encampment at Paran the setting for the great trial of faith to come, the sending of the spies, vv.13); the journey resumed after the chastening (the people moving on) — the journey (from Hazeroth); 'pitched in the wilderness of Paran' (the camp at Paran, the place of the spies, Numbers 13:3, the figure, the pilgrimage resumed, the setting for the trial to come, the providence, the camp at Paran, the spies to be sent, ch. 13) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And afterward the people removed from Hazeroth, and pitched in the wilderness of Paran

the journey continued after the discipline, the people moving on under God's guidance
